purpose: Externally sourced doctrine inputs for AI analysis and future strategy-card expansion.

research_items.title: Order flow imbalance and depth outweigh raw volume
core_claims: Short-horizon price change is explained more robustly by order-flow imbalance than by raw volume alone.
Market depth changes the impact of one-sided flow.
Flow toxicity matters because liquidity providers can be adversely selected.
ai_implications: Downgrade large prints without meaningful imbalance or price progress.
Rank imbalance plus depth context above isolated volume spikes.
no_trade_implications: Do not open from high volume alone when price is not progressing.
Do not treat strong delta as sufficient if the book remains balanced.

research_items.title: Post-shock resiliency decides continuation versus repair
core_claims: Spread, depth, and order intensity often normalize quickly after liquidity shocks.
The post-shock recovery pattern differs by aggressiveness of the initiating order.
Continuation versus repair is visible after the hit, not only during the hit.
ai_implications: Use the first pullback after a burst as a major diagnostic checkpoint.
Require proof that gained ground is still defended after the initial shock.
no_trade_implications: Do not chase the first burst before the book shows whether it is rebuilding or repairing.
Do not assume the shock created durable control without post-shock hold quality.

research_items.title: Persistent one-way prints can be absorbed by adaptive liquidity
core_claims: Predictable one-way order flow does not mechanically imply the same price response.
Opposite-side liquidity can adapt as directional predictability rises.
ai_implications: Downgrade repeated same-side prints if price response is deteriorating.
Treat persistence without progress as a warning branch.
no_trade_implications: Do not keep adding just because prints remain one-way.
Do not confuse persistent aggression with clean continuation if travel efficiency is shrinking.

research_items.title: Stop clusters can create cascades without proving new acceptance
core_claims: Stop-loss clusters can trigger rapid and lasting price cascades.
Non-informative order flow can still move price materially.
ai_implications: Model stop-release bursts as a separate event from accepted new auction.
Require post-cascade acceptance or exhaustion checks.
no_trade_implications: Do not assume a stop cascade proves continuation.
Do not fade every cascade immediately without seeing whether outside trade is accepted.

research_items.title: Displayed size can be deceptive and must be verified
core_claims: False market depth is a recognized spoofing-related behavior.
A recurring pattern is genuine interest on one side and larger displayed orders on the other side that are canceled after fills.
ai_implications: Treat displayed size as untrusted until survival near touch and refill behavior are proven.
Bias toward visual bait when large size disappears as price approaches.
no_trade_implications: Do not open solely because a large order appears near the top of book.
Do not label flashing and pulling size as same-price replenishment.

research_items.title: Passive liquidity behavior is inventory- and toxicity-sensitive
core_claims: Liquidity providers manage inventory risk and adverse-selection risk.
Passive liquidity may retreat or reprice when conditions become toxic.
ai_implications: Separate defense, retreat, inventory offload, and re-quote branches.
Do not overread passive size as directional conviction.
no_trade_implications: Do not trust static DOM size when adverse-selection pressure rises.
Do not label retreating liquidity as a clean failure until the post-retreat branch is visible.

research_items.title: Cross-market leadership is dynamic and liquidity-dependent
core_claims: Lead-lag relationships change over time and are not permanently fixed.
Comparative liquidity and order-book context help decide which market is clearer.
ai_implications: Treat current anchor leadership as a state, not a permanent symbol identity.
Prefer the market with cleaner acceptance or rejection at the decision point.
no_trade_implications: Do not force ES or NQ into permanent leadership roles.
Do not trust lagging confirmation if the prior leader already degraded.

research_items.title: Initial-balance logic is useful but must stay contextual
core_claims: IB breakout, extension, and day-type logic depend on session framing.
The source warns against using such statistics blindly.
ai_implications: Keep IB breakout, IB extension, and accepted outside trade as separate labels.
Use IB as a context filter rather than a standalone signal.
no_trade_implications: Do not open from IB statistics alone without session-specific validation.
Do not call every push beyond IB a true breakout.

candidate_strategy_cards.why_high_value: It converts many dramatic events into a single practical question: did the book rebuild for continuation or repair back toward balance?
It separates stop-driven extension from accepted new value and directly improves no-chase discipline.
It would sharply improve AI handling of visible size and reduce false support or resistance promotion.
It targets a common false-opportunity error: strong flow labels that never become real travel.
It keeps IB statistics from being overused and improves opening-session context quality.
It explains why passive size can vanish or reprice without fitting naive support or resistance stories.
